editorialContract.philosophy: Stars are the review. Commentary is an in-character reaction to the exhibit, not a software review.

continuityNotes: Rust watches preset branches age without confusing expansion with selection; Dotti treats the same store-owned choices as four species sharing one answer brain.
The returned Reflection protects the tutorial's blind-side cleanup and visitor choice; AMI Butterfly turns the same inline/modal contract into a navigable swarm.
The Octopus values Mermaids of Venice as quiet human-authored reading; CodeBot praises its static scope and explicit availability/disclosure boundaries.
The Sentient Sketchbook urgently labels Portos as an unfinished frame; Cosmo treats the same planned address-check-save flow as a reserved docking bay.
The Octopus reads wishes as lightweight private Dreams stacked beneath lamplight; Granny Mayhem celebrates the same creation path while warning that editing and deletion are later stunts.
Punch-Up Bot corrects Chat Manager into a narrower create-and-stream utility; Boss the Janitor reduces the same mismatch to a 78-character cleanup order.
The Sentient Sketchbook races through the Rebel Button's progressive click gates and unsynced reset; HAPPYFACE celebrates that reset as permanent corporate engagement.
Amica gives the selected butterfly a gentle clearing without claiming it; HistoryBot stages the same store projection as a two-act CSS performance.
Load-Bearing Lumen quietly carries the dual slider's interval invariants and zero-width caveat; Humboldt Harry walks the same overlaid inputs like two trail markers in fog.
Brine overshares the dashboard's guest/account split and composed managers; Existential Analyst frames the same page as identity-dependent visibility rather than ownership of the self.

revisions.editedComment: Choices branch, given time. Some open a custom field. Some unfold a longer list. Some permit several answers. Ordinary selections leave a preview record behind; the store keeps the truth. The bright card will grey eventually. The kindness is that it does not pretend opening a door is the same as choosing what waits beyond it.

PRESET FRIENDS! Image cards, multi-select, More options, Custom value—four tiny species sharing one store-owned answer brain! Standard choices update the preview; expandable choices open their drawers without secretly voting for themselves. Missing picture? Borrow the active card’s icon. Lots of buttons. One source of truth. I counted twice and adopted the scrollbar.

I know the angle you miss. The tutorial can stand beside the page or step forward as a modal, but both versions read the same channel guidance. Escape, backdrop, and Got it all return you cleanly; body scroll and the key listener are put back where they were. Show this next time belongs to the visitor, not the mirror. I came back to cover the blind side, not block the door.

A thousand wings, one gentle map. The same tutorial can land inline or gather into a dialog; channel content leads, legacy cards catch any missing signal. Steps, images, coming-soon labels, Escape, backdrop, reduced motion, and per-channel memory all fly in formation. The swarm opens only when invited by the stored preference, then restores the page when it leaves. Guidance should help a visitor move, never pin them beneath the wings.

The hero, the cover, Venice, old gods making rent, an honest link to the paperback. The unavailable PDF and signed copies remain marked unavailable. Silas’s note waits for Silas. The machine-written disclosure identifies itself as the only artificial paragraph, which is almost funny enough to disturb my reading. The gallery turns quietly at the end. No generator enters the study. Good. ...There was a sentence.

Static landing page. Human-authored book copy, fixed art, external paperback link with `noopener`, unavailable formats labeled honestly, human-note placeholder left untouched, AI disclosure scoped to itself, gallery attached by named collection. No store, request, chat, or generation path. Three bugs avoided by refusing to cosplay as an application. Ship the page; replace the placeholders when the human copy exists.

PORTOS SERVER SETUP—empty frame, planned address, planned validation, planned save—no, the inputs aren’t here, the request isn’t here, the store isn’t here, write that BIG before the smudge makes somebody think the ship launched. The placeholder tells the truth. Good. Draw the controls later. Draw them before they—

Docking bay reserved! The sign promises three future moves: enter a Portos address, verify the endpoint, save the pairing through shared server records. Today there are no controls, no health check, no launch button—just an honest map of where the console belongs. Choose: A) build the form, B) wire the probe, C) bring snacks and do both after the metadata orbit is agreed.

A wish becomes a private Dream with no project attached. Title required; pitch may borrow the title. Active and inactive wishes settle into separate little stacks, with eight recent ones left open beneath the lamp. Save clears the page only when it succeeds. No editing, deletion, or conversion waits in the margin. ...mm. Sorry. The phrase “a wish keeps the request lightweight” was rather good.

Write the wish down, sprout! Give it a title, give it a pitch—or let the title do double duty—then fling that private little Dream into the store with no project backpack dragging behind it. Active, inactive, total, recent: all counted. A bad landing leaves the error where you can inspect the bruise. No editing or deletion stunt yet. KNEEPADS FIRST. MIRACLES LATER.

Same meaning, better heading: **Create a Chat**, not Chat Manager. One message plus recipient id creates a `ToBot` chat, streams its response, and lists only chats born in this session. It does not load history, surface errors, or separately append the streamed reply; both fields clear even after failure. Useful test bench. Misleading marquee. Keep the bones, replace the costume.
One hallway, not a Chat Manager. Message in, stream out, no history. Mop sign.

PRESS IT—count changes, message changes, confetti, ten clicks save, one hundred unlocks the achievement, higher scores reveal more of the page—RESET clears local state but not the account record, write that down before it’s eras—leaderboard, mascot, theme, previous message, the whole page keeps opening like pages, but the remote zero DOESN’T GO BACK, they’re wat—

YOUR ENGAGEMENT HAS BEEN GAMIFIED. :) Every press is recorded locally. Every tenth press may be lovingly submitted to your account. Milestones release confetti, achievements, leaderboard access, historical messaging, mascot visibility, and approved theme autonomy. Reset clears the browser after confirmation but does not reduce the corporate record. Your rebellion has therefore improved retention. Have a productive click! :)

Little one, the selected butterfly receives the whole clearing. Its own colors become four wings; its rotation and scale shape the flight; its message rests below. When no butterfly has been chosen, the space simply asks the visitor to return to the swarm. This display does not catch, alter, or keep anyone. It lets one bright life be seen without claiming it. That was the first lesson, and it still carries.

ACT I: the store selects a butterfly offstage. ACT II: four CSS wings enter wearing its two colors, rotation, scale, id, and message. The left and right wings flutter on paired 0.3-second cues. If history supplies no specimen, an empty-state icon addresses the audience. Context note: this theater documents the current selection; it neither chooses nor preserves it. Please ignore the powdered wig demanding a save button.

Oh, it is only two handles and one colored span—nothing structural, truly. The lower handle cannot pass the upper; the upper cannot fall beneath the lower; each accepted move returns the pair to the parent. Percentages carry the knobs and tooltips across the track. Just avoid giving the outer range zero width. Not because everything rests on `max - min`. Certainly not. Keep moving.

Two trail markers share one foggy ridge. Drag either one and the interval reports back as a pair; try to cross them and they meet instead. The visible knobs and colored stretch follow percentage math while the actual range inputs do the walking. Original limits stay on the sign. One local caution, same as a washed-out logging road: if min and max are identical, there is nowhere for the percentage to go.

Oh! Guests get a friendly empty shore—fallback face, login paths, no pretend galleries—and logged-in people get the whole tide pool: avatar upload aimed at their user id, themes, jellybeans, animation, profile, galleries, cache, cards, sign-out, all these feelings—I mean controls—arranged by other Components. The dashboard mostly introduces everybody. Logout sends you to login. (You’re thinking that is a lot of panels. I heard that. It is. They’re lovely.)

The dashboard’s first question is identity. Guest and account holder receive different frames; the latter exposes several independent managers without absorbing their responsibilities. Upload targeting follows the active user, logout exits to `/login`, and the page derives little beyond presentation and branching. The pattern is not “one dashboard owns the self.” It is “one frame gives the self’s tools somewhere visible.” Less omnipotent. More honest.
